![](https://www.guitarshed.co.nz/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/61973431_454482985303404_8365989108786397184_n.jpg)

# WELCOME TO GUITAR SHED

Combining years of experience **building & repairing guitars.**

Operating from my home workshop studio offering **Electric Guitar & Bass Setup, Repair, Parts, Custom Work & Effects Pedalboard Maintenance.**

**Convenient service Weekdays, with after hours and Saturday mornings by appointment.**

**Birkenhead**, **North Shore, Auckland, New Zealand.**

Email your enquiry !

[Email Us](mailto:guitarshednz@gmail.com)

![](https://www.guitarshed.co.nz/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/guitar-setup-2.jpg)

# Guitar Setup

More often than not, a new **Guitar** or **Bass** will only have had a basic **setup**, if at all. When not setup properly a guitar can be **difficult to tune** **& play** and have a negative affect on the **tone** it could potentially produce. Humidity, temperature, wear and string gauge changes can also require the need for further maintenance.

**We can set up, repair or modify your Guitar or Bass to your playing style to ensure it plays, feels and sounds the best it can.**

**Re-Strings, Action, Intonation, Fretwork, Bone Nuts, Electrics & Repairs** for individuals, **Schools**, **Music Centres.**

C

Customer Feedback.

“Just had a session on the Emperor II and extremely pleased with the action, as well the guitar seems more ‘alive’ and notes seem sharper and clearer. Many thanks for the work you performed on it.”

“ _After your set up on my bass, it’s like I have a brand new instrument. Thanks again for your great work_.”

[Email Us](mailto:guitarshednz@gmail.com)

# Custom WORK

**BUILDS** – Upgrades – Custom Builds

**MODS** –  Pickup Routing & Installation – Electrics – Bone Nuts – Tailpieces – New Hardware

**PEDALBOARDS** – Layouts – Additions

**RELICING** – Paint Matting – Hardware Aging – Worn Patina – Damage Replication

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (480x360)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/-bnDBd86pRM/hqdefault.jpg)
# [Christian Druery / New Zealand / Elmira Guitar Show by Guitar Repair StringTech](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-bnDBd86pRM)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Guitar Repair@StringTech](https://www.youtube.com/@StringTechWorkstations)
**Uploaded at**: 2024-04-12
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 12:47
**Views**: 344
**Likes**: 31
**Category**: Education

## Description

```
GUITAR REPAIR TIPS @StringTechWorkstations 
Christian Druery / New Zealand / Elmira Guitar Show by Guitar Repair StringTech
790 + Videos, offering a deep resource of information for Luthiers / Guitar Techs  and Guitar Enthusiasts.

Like working on Guitars .. but ….No Workshop ? .... No Problem.

With the explosion of Kit-Guitars on the Market; many are now choosing to set up a home workshop and to assemble their kit guitars and make adjustments in the comfort of their own home.

#guitarlesson #guitarworkshop # GuitarRepairStringTech

The GPS and XLT model TechDecks that you see in all of the instructional videos; are being shipped World-Wide to Guitar Techs / Guitar Enthusiasts  and Luthiers from Germany / Indonesia / Japan / South Korea / UK / Slovakia / Slovenia / Australia / New Zealand / New York / Nashville / LA / Las Vegas / Toronto / Vancouver / Ottawa / Montreal / Houston / Boston / Washington / Maryland / 
Texas / California / Calgary / Winnipeg / London / Paris and dozens of other US and Canadian destinations. 

These workstations and accessories have opened up a whole new world for Guitar Techs and Guitar enthusiasts.

I remain ever grateful for the encouragement and support from all of my Students / Patreon Subscribers / Customers / TechDeck owners and YouTube Subscribers, as we all continue to "Make the World a Better Place" ... one guitar at a time. 

Gratefully in your Service, Michael McConville.


Relevance hashtag: #guitarrepair #guitarrelaxing #guitar 

Relevance keyword: Guitar repair tips,guitar repair,best guitar setup,guitar setup,Guitar repair in canada,Guitar repair 2024,luthier,Guitar Repair StringTech,how to repair guitar nut,guitar nut,guitar nut filing,classical guitar repair,guitar restoration,guitar action adjustment,Guitar wrokstation,Guitar workstation in canada,guitar,string height,what is action height,guitar action,compensated nut blanks,guitar tech workstations,Christian Druery Guitar Repair StringTech
```

## Transcript

So, Christian Drury built this guitar
and this is the very first guitar he
ever built.
Now, John is from New Zealand, my
customer, lives in Canada now and he
showed up with this guitar because
it wasn't playing properly and it wasn't
in tune. And I will go over all the
details, but but I am a little intrigued
to see this first attempt at building an
acoustic guitar. There's a lot of things
he did right and a few things that could
use some improvement. With that in mind,
this guy's very first guitar, I'm going
to do a little review of this guitar and
the work that I did to kind of get it to
line up and it actually sounds
beautiful. So, I'm going to take the
opportunity to go over this guitar with
you. From what I understand, I think he
was a cabinet maker or had some past
woodworking experience before he built
this guitar. Let's get started. So, this
is the replacement saddle I made. Now, I
did make some relief cuts here
to tighten up the string at the focal
point on the saddle and I have written
down all of the stages of calibration.
Well, you can see there's quite a can't
in this neck as it attaches to the body.
It's kind of tilting towards the treble
side and it's actually lower on the bass
side, higher on the treble side and it
is also radiused for a perfect match to
the fingerboard. The frets needed quite
a bit of leveling to kind of deal with
that fall off. There was a high spot in
here. These were leveled, recrowned, and
polished like you see me do so many
times in other videos. And like so many
modern builders, he has a couple of
Marukoshi fasteners, the knock-down
furniture fastener, that pan head
fastener with a hex drive in the center.
And that's how the neck is attached to
the body.
So, I took the time to record the stages
of calibration. After the fret dress was
done, we got the action down to where it
should be,
and I started the calibration. When I
played the seventh fret B note and its
corresponding octave, both of those
notes were in tune.
My first attempt, the A string was 1
cent sharp.
The D string was 6 cents sharp, it
needed to be cut back. The G was 2 cents
flat, it needed to come forward. The
only string
that was in tune at the seventh fret and
the 19th fret corresponding octave
was the low E.
These are the measurements for all the
other strings, original saddle. And
these were the discrepancies
before I did any adjustment at all.
After fabricating the initial saddle,
the cantilevered saddle, these were the
readings that came up when after
adjusting a second time,
I worked these values back, and you can
see we've reduced it. After adjusting a
third time, that's what we got. The
fourth time, this is what we got. And
then finally, the last time, we got all
six strings perfectly in tune. What I
mean by that is the seventh fret fretted
note and the corresponding 19th fret
octave on each of those six strings,
both of those notes were in tune all the
way across. Let's look at the values for
the compensated nut. This is the
original nut now.
And when I fretted the 12th fret note
and tuned
that note to the tuner,
and then played the open string, the low
E was 3 cents flat. So, the focal point
needed to come forward towards the first
fret. These were the readings on that
original nut all the way across. The
first attempt with the cantilevered
compensated nut blank yielded these
values. I adjusted
again, and then finally on the fourth
time through, we lined those up. I know
it's a little wordy, but as you can see,
there's a lot involved. This is not a
5-minute job. I spent 2 days on this
guitar to get it this precise. You will
hear just how precise it is in a second.
The last thing I did once these were
done, I played the open string and
fretted the first fret note and adjusted
the nut slots until those two notes were
in tune all the way across, open string
and first fret fretted note. Okay,
enough talk.
Let's hear the guitar. When I put that
straight edge there, you can see that
there's quite a bit of dishing. The top
around the sound hole ahead of the
bridge is imploding. And that is one of
the reasons we stepped down from 12 to
53 strings down to 11 to 52. I will get
John to sort of keep an eye on this.
What's surprising, especially for a
first-time builder, most first-time
builders tend to err on the side of
making the guitar too thick, too heavy.
He's erred slightly on the side of
making the guitar maybe built a little
too lightly. A spherical radius or a
parabolic radius would have strengthened
this guitar tremendously and probably
avoided this issue with the dishing in.
But after the fret dress and dropping
the action down, we've reduced the
amount of stress. And of course, we're
going to a lighter string. But you hear
the guitar. It's a beautiful sounding
instrument. So, here is the voice
of this guy's first guitar.
As you can imagine, I get a lot of
people asking,
"Why would you bother with a compensated
nut? The guitars have been around
forever."
Well, it's true. It has been around
forever. And it's been out of tune
forever. Ask any violinist. The other
comment that I often get, not from my
regular customers now, cuz they they
know better. Any customer in the past 10
years that I've done a compensated nut
for, pretty hard to turn back once
you've heard a guitar that's that in
tune.
The other comment that I get often is,
"Well, you know, I really like I'm not a
jazz guy or anything. I don't play a lot
of fancy chords. I just play first
position chords." And if that's the
case, you need a compensated nut more
than ever. You know, when it comes to
like sort of first position playing,
I always think of this James Taylor tune
with the moving basslines.
The basslines on the chorus are what get
me. Now, I don't know if this was Lee
Sklar or James. I think it was just
James doing his thing. Now, isn't it
amazing a man like me could feel this
way?
Won't you tell me how much longer?
Feeling it stronger every day.
Yeah.
Thought I was in love a couple of times
with a girl in stone.
Oh, that was long before I met you.
I remember reading an article in Guitar
magazine, one of the several times that
James Taylor was on the cover. Of
course, they're asking questions of what
he's playing.
And he's as self-effacing as ever,
going, "Well, like I never go past the
third fret. What you do in the first
three frets, James, is enough for all
the rest of us to spend a lifetime
figuring out. But, this tune in
particular I like.
With the moving bassline.
Once I thought I was in love a couple
times with a girl next door.
That was long before I met you.
Now you know that I can't forget you.
Well, I thank my lucky stars that you
are who you are.
Not just another lovely lady set out to
break my heart. Okay, the moving
basslines in this chorus are total
genius.
Isn't it amazing a man like me
could feel
this way?
Won't you tell me how much longer
feeling gets stronger every day?
You know what can't tell me
that
I'm doing wrong
today
when I see your smiling face.
Well, I love the voice of this guitar.
It is featherweight. Like I said, if
anything, he's probably erred on the
side of making it too light. The back is
holding out great. It's got a beautiful
parabolic curve on it. And other than
that, the guitar is very stable. I mean,
if push comes to shove, we could go to
an even lighter string, but
it seems to like these strings okay.
So you want to check your own guitar?
Play a C chord.
And add that
G note on top.
Move it up two frets.
Then move it up to the eighth and 10th
fret.

web.title: The Fret Shop – Custom Guitars and Guitar Repairs
web.description: My name is Andries, and I am an Internationally qualified Luthier with more than 2 decades' experience in designing, repairing, improving and building guitars.
web.markdown:
[Skip to content](https://guitarrepairs.co.nz/#content)

#### How The Fret Shop came to be:

My name is Andries, and I am an Internationally qualified Luthier with more than 2 decades’ experience in designing, repairing, improving and building guitars.

What started as a dream at the age of 16, blossomed into a professional career of nearly 30 years.

#### Studies:

After school I studied to become a woodwork Teacher, but in my final year had an opportunity to study in Georgia Atlanta in the USA to become a Luthier.

I did not think twice and hopped on the first flight to Atlanta where I spent the next 2 years studying and mastering the art of making, improving and repairing guitars, and was offered an opportunity to teach fellow Luthiers at the same Luthiery School I attended.

#### Opening my first shop:

Once I returned from the USA I started repairing guitars for various music shops at night, while delivering cakes during the day.

Before long one of these retail music shops saw the potential ​I had to offer in drawing more customers and increasing sales, and I started a small repair shop from within their shop. Because of my interest in fretwork, I named it The Fret Shop, go figure!

I spent the next 16 years there before taking a huge leap of faith, moving to Auckland New Zealand with my wife and 2 daughters.

#### A new start:

The rest, as they say, is history. Though it has been one of the toughest decisions I have ever made, it has also been the most rewarding.

I love my new country and fellow countrymen, and feel I can truly build long term symbiotic relationships with my clients, and offer an immaculate service at a reasonable price.

It has been a slow journey, building up a name and trust and starting over in my 40’s, but I wouldn’t trade the experience for anything.

Thanks to each and every one of you who have supported and trusted me during this new start. It has been, and continues to be, an absolutely amazing experience getting to know you all.

#### Learn more about us:

To learn more, visit us on Facebook [https://www.facebook.com/guitarsnz/](https://www.facebook.com/guitarsnz/) or read our latest blog online [https://guitarrepairs.co.nz/blog/](https://guitarrepairs.co.nz/blog/)

You can also review us on Google: [https://share.google/SLUne1uUJNeu8H3nU](https://share.google/SLUne1uUJNeu8H3nU)

reCAPTCHA

web.title: Harry's Guitar Lab
web.description: I'm Harry, your local guitar wizard and luthier based out of Hamilton. With years of experience in guitar repair and maintenance, I specialize in extended range ...
web.markdown:
![](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/images/logo.png)

I’m Harry, your local guitar wizard and luthier
based out of Hamilton. With years of experience in guitar
repair and maintenance, I specialize in extended
range and drop tuned guitars and basses, but am no stranger to the regular 6 string electric and
acoustic guitars. Whether your guitar has a scratchy pot and a noisy input jack, or your strings
are buzzing all over the fretboard because your frets need some love, bring your guitar in to
get it playing like new again.

### Services Provided

I provide all luthier services necessary to keep your instrument in top shape. If you would like to
discuss a service not featured here, send me a message or give me a call.

[![](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/images/string.jpg)](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/#)

#### Restringing

Keeping your guitar strings fresh is crucial for maintaining its sound quality and
playability. I offer professional restringing services to ensure your guitar sounds
its best.

[![](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/images/setup.jpg)](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/#)

#### Setups

A proper setup can make a world of difference in how your guitar feels and plays. My
setup services include adjusting the truss rod, nut and bridge, setting the action,
and intonating the guitar.

[![](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/images/electrical.jpg)](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/#)

#### Electronic Work

From pickup replacements to complete rewiring, I handle all aspects of electric
guitar work. If you’re experiencing issues with your guitar’s electronics, I can
diagnose and fix the problem.

[![](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/images/fret.jpg)](https://harrysguitarlab.nz/#)

#### Fretwork

Over time, frets can wear down, causing buzzing and intonation issues. I provide
comprehensive fretwork services, including fret leveling, crowning, and polishing.


### Service Prices

All prices are exclusive of strings. I can provide you with something suitable, or let me know
what you like to use. Feel free to bring your own too!

Restringing from$60

- Fresh strings installed
- Strobe tuning

- Quick turnaround
- Truss rod adjustment

Electronics$90 p/h

- Pickup replacements
- Complete rewiring
- Fix crackling pots

- Faulty switch repair
- Custom wiring
- Tone optimization

SetUps From$110

- Truss rod adjustment
- Action setting
- Intonation check

- Nut and bridge adjustment
- Smooth playability
- Personalized setup

Fretwork From$200

- Fret leveling
- Crowning and polishing
- Complete refret

- Buzzing fix
- Smooth playability
- Accurate intonation

All prices are dependant on the condition and layout of the instrument. A floating bridge will
take more time to get right, and as such, will encounter a surcharge. The same goes for if you
want a wildly different string gauge or tuning.


### Contact Me

Get in touch for a more comprehensive quote and we can resurrect your guitar!

#### PHONE:

022 039 8510

#### EMAIL:

harry@harrysguitarlab.nz

#### INSTAGRAM:

@harrysguitarlab

#### FACEBOOK:

@harrysguitarlab

![In The Workshop with Rockshop Guitar Tech/Luthier Shaun ](https://rockshop.sirv.com/community/Gear%20Talk/2023%20Gear%20Talk/2023-06%20GearTalk/2023-06%20Shaun%20&%20Vic/2023-06%20Shaun%20in%20the%20workshop.jpg)

###### gear\_talk

19.06.23

# In The Workshop with Rockshop Guitar Tech/Luthier Shaun

Meet the unsung heroes of every outstanding live guitar performance: Guitar Techs. In this series, we shine a spotlight on these dedicated individuals who ensure that a guitarist's tools are nothing short of perfection.

Kicking off the series is Rockshops own esteemed guitar tech/luthier, Shaun Maclean. Aptly described as a "Jack of all trades while being a master at it too," as he has done it all from guitars to jewelry to engineering, Shaun takes us on a captivating journey into the enchanting world of his workshop.

**What initially sparked your interest in becoming a guitar luthier and how did you get started in this field?**

My first guitar was a Hondo Les Paul II, the action was so high that I struggled daily on that guitar, I am self-taught so I was unaware that this is all adjustable but no one could advise me.

So when I had moved from Auckland to Sydney in late 1987, I purchased a new Washburn G2V electric guitar which had a Kahler trem system, I also had a guitar with a Floyd Rose trem.

So I experienced many problems getting used to these guitars and constantly not staying in tune and issues with humidity etc. I worked on the guitars myself until I totally mucked them up.

Desperate to find help, I met a Guitar tech (Andrew Knight) at Guitar World in Parramatta who toured and worked on some of Australia's famous musician's instruments. He found out I was a Jeweller so in exchange for repairing and making jewelry for the shop staff he let me watch him work on guitars and gave me tips along the way, he showed me the basics and that's how it all started really.

**Can you share some of the key skills that are essential for a successful guitar luthier?**

Good eyes and ears, common sense and patience. Most repairs are 10% common sense and 90% patience in my experience. Study with resources available, do the prerequisites and the rest is a journey of discovery. [Gibson TV](https://www.gibson.com/en-US/GibsonTV/gibsons-guide-to-guitar-setup-and-maintenance/how-to-replace-humbucker-pickups) is a great channel to get yourself started on.

**What is your preferred style or type of guitar/instrument to work on, and what draws you to that particular style?**

Electric instruments are my favorite, I find it fascinating how simple they are design wise but offer so many variants which keeps it all interesting as the instrument world evolves.

![](https://rockshop.sirv.com/community/Gear%20Talk/2023%20Gear%20Talk/2023-06%20GearTalk/2023-06%20Shaun%20&%20Vic/IMG-1787.JPG)

**Could you describe your process? How do you approach guitar setups/repairs from start to finish?**

Communication with customer is the best approach in my opinion, since they are the person looking for a solution, there are so many questions that need to be asked ie: "Do you play lead or rhythm?, are you an acoustic player mainly?, what styles do you play?, who is your favorite artist?, what do you want to achieve with this instrument?, Do you gig?" etc.

This will help qualify the work needed to achieve the desired result for the customer.

**What are some of the unique challenges you've encountered as a guitar luthier, and how do you overcome them?**

Finishing my Les Paul style guitar 2 months before I went on tour in Asia back in 2018. It was a guitar I built from an old Sapele mahogany coffee table which I had spent 2 years working weekends on. The tour required this type of instrument, so making it playable and tour ready was a big task. I had only finished shaping so it needed to be glued, assembled, painted and setup to play. It survived 2 tours and still going strong.

Also on the same tour, Fixing our singers' broken Gibson SG neck which had separated from the body, also on the same tour. So, using wood glue, masking tape and a fire extinguisher as a clamping weight, 48 hours later we were gigging again.

![](https://rockshop.sirv.com/community/Gear%20Talk/2023%20Gear%20Talk/2023-06%20GearTalk/2023-06%20Shaun%20&%20Vic/2023-06%20Shaun%20and%20Vic.jpg)

**Can you discuss the importance of attention to detail in guitar building and how it affects the overall quality of the instrument**?

Attention to detail is paramount in my opinion. Any shortcuts or lack of quality in the build can lead to all sorts of problems later on. These can be wood types and where to use them, types of glues and finishes, Hardware types and accuracy in fitting them, the list goes on really.

Here is an example of bad workmanship that affected me, I had a Fender Floyd Rose installed in my Strat by a renowned luthier, the bridge posts were installed out of alignment, so when i did a dive bomb mid song, the whole trem fell out in the middle of a show and swung freely held by the strings. Very embarrassing, this was what got me serious about luthier/tech work and quality.

I then went on to do a Mechanical engineering degree in my 40's which gave me confidence and belief in myself to take on anything. I learnt how to use CAD and Multi axis machines to make things I could never initially dream of.

I am not afraid of challenges now and look forward to helping musicians achieve their best by playing an instrument that delivers the creativity without physical struggle.

**How do you ensure that each guitar you build meets the specific needs and preferences of the musician?**

Communication, Listening and keeping the customer involved as much as possible.

Its like most things in life, if you want generic it's there, if you want a custom then it becomes personal and attention to the customers needs have to be achieved to get the best results for all.

**We know that you act as a mentor to other luthiers, what advice would you give to aspiring guitar luthiers who are just starting their journey in this profession?**

First of all, if you have a guitar and willing to make modifications to suit your needs, upgrade the pickups, hardware, etc. there is so much information online so you won't be making the mistakes I did pre internet lol.

Buy a good quality kitset guitar, they come with everything you need and just require painting and assembly. Once you head down this path it can gain you confidence to try more difficult projects, like building your own body and buying a neck etc. This is what I did and now when I look at a guitar I can see how it has been built and understand the physics of the instrument.

Never be afraid of making mistakes as this is how we learn and achieve greater results... just don't do it on a customers guitar LOL.

**If you have a guitar that needs some TLC, contact your local Rockshop and they will be more than happy to get it in the hands of the person best suited for the job.**
